turns.text: Hello Harriet, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s funny, I spend my whole day at work telling other parents that they’re doing a great job, that those developmental milestones are just markers, not grades. I’m the calm one in the room, the one who helps them read their baby’s cues. But then I come home, and I feel like I’ve left my common sense at the door.

Being pregnant again… it’s strange. I look at this bump, and part of me is just so grateful. It’s a miracle, it really is. But the other part? It’s just waiting for the other shoe to drop. My OB sees "low risk" on the chart, and in their head, I’m sure that means a boring, standard pregnancy. But my body remembers the magnesium, the beeping monitors, and the way the room turned grey before the seizures started.

It’s hard to talk to the doctors about it. I know the terminology, so I use it. I think that makes them treat me like a colleague rather than a patient, which means they miss the fact that I’m vibrating with anxiety every time I check for swelling in my ankles. I just want someone to look at me—not the chart, not the "low risk" box they checked—and just tell me they’re watching, too. Does that make sense? It’s a very lonely kind of fear.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

It’s a complicated dance, isn’t it? With my OB, I feel this strange pressure to perform. Because I work in early childhood development, I show up to my appointments with my "professional" face on. I speak the language, I nod at the right times, and I try to keep it together so they don't see me as the "anxious, difficult patient." But honestly? That keeps us at arm's length. They see my history of eclampsia as a box that’s been checked, a past event that doesn't define the "now." They’re quick to tell me everything is standard, and while I’m relieved to hear that, it feels like they’re glossing over the fact that "standard" is exactly what I was last time, right up until the point my life went sideways.

I find myself wishing for someone to just sit with me for a minute—not to check my blood pressure and move to the next patient, but to acknowledge that I’m carrying this weight. My husband is my rock, he really is, but he’s carrying his own trauma from when he watched me on that hospital bed. I don't want to burden him with the hourly check of my wedding ring. I need my care team to be the ones who say, "I see you, and I remember." I don't need them to be therapists, I just need them to not be so clinical. Everything feels so hollow when the focus is only on the numbers.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

My husband, Mark, tries so hard. He’s the one who rubs my back when I’m spiraling about a headache or a bit of lightheadedness, and he’s the one who keeps the house steady when I’m too drained from work to even think about dinner. But I see the look in his eyes sometimes—that specific, wide-eyed terror—when I mention even a small symptom. We both have this vivid, permanent recording of that room in the hospital playing in our heads.

Because he remembers it just as clearly as I do, I’ve started hiding things from him. If my ring feels a little tight, I’ll pull it off and tuck it in my pocket before he notices. I don't want to be the reason he’s panicking, and I don't want him to be the reason *I* panic. It’s become this strange, unspoken pact where we both act like everything is perfectly fine because we’re both too scared to poke the bear.

When he comes to appointments, it’s a relief to have his hand in mine, but it’s also heavier. We’re sitting there in the quiet of the exam room, both holding our breath, waiting for the doctor to give us the "all clear." It’s like we’re waiting for a verdict every single time. His presence is my safety net, but it’s also a constant reminder of how much we have to lose.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

It’s a double-edged sword, really. On one hand, there’s an intimacy in what we’ve been through. We share a secret language of trauma that no one else in our life can truly grasp. When we look at each other across the dinner table, we don't have to say a word; we both know exactly which memory is surfacing. That kind of shared history usually pulls people tight, and in a way, it has. We are very protective of one another.

But there is a tension, too. It’s like we’re walking on eggshells inside our own home. I’ve started filtering my worries so I don’t trigger his fear, and I know he does the same for me. Sometimes I feel like we’re acting in a play where the script is "The Perfect Pregnancy," and if either of us breaks character, the whole thing might come crashing down. 

I’ll see him watching me when he thinks I’m not looking, just checking to see if I’m having a headache or if my face looks swollen. It makes me feel seen, but it also makes me feel like a patient in my own living room. We’re closer, yes, but we’re both exhausted from the effort of pretending that we aren't terrified. It’s a lot to ask of a marriage, to be a partner and a stabilizer at the same time.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

Honestly? I’ve turned to almost no one. It’s isolating, being in my position. My friends who haven't had a traumatic birth experience—which is most of them—just don't get it. They mean well, but they offer platitudes like, "Oh, every pregnancy is different!" or "You're so lucky it's a low-risk one this time." They think they're being comforting, but it just makes me feel like I’m crazy for being scared. It makes me feel like I’m ungrateful for this baby, which is the last thing I am.

As for online communities, I’ve dipped my toes into some forums, but I usually end up closing the laptop pretty quickly. Seeing other people’s horror stories just feeds my own worst-case scenarios, and the "everything will be perfect" groups feel so disconnected from my reality. 

It leaves me in this strange middle ground where I’m working professionally with parents all day, being the voice of reason and support for *their* anxieties, but I have no safe harbor for my own. My formal care team sees the "low risk" label, and my friends see the "happy pregnant woman" label. I keep my real fears locked away because nobody really wants to hear the messy, terrifying truth of what it’s like to survive something once and dread having to survive it all over again. I’m just navigating this in the dark, really.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

Early in this pregnancy, there was a discussion about whether I should be on low-dose aspirin. Because of my history, it’s a standard recommendation for preeclampsia prevention, but I had this moment of pure, blinding panic. I kept thinking, "If I take this, does it mean it’s definitely going to happen again? Am I just postponing the inevitable?"

It was just Mark and me in the kitchen when we talked about it. I was crying, which isn't like me—I’m the one who keeps it together at work—but I felt so powerless. Mark was the one who actually pushed for it. He didn't want to leave anything to chance. He said, "Harriet, if there’s even a one percent chance this keeps you safe, we have to do it." His input hit me hard because it wasn't just about the science; it was his plea to keep me here, with him.

When I brought it up to my OB, I didn't frame it as a question. I framed it as a "I read this, and I want it." They agreed immediately, which was validating, but it also made me feel like I had to be my own advocate for the simplest things. It’s exhausting, needing to be the one to push for basic safeguards just so I can feel like I have some agency over my own body again.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

My work environment is the biggest paradox. I’m a professional in early childhood, so I’m surrounded by the joy of newborns and developmental milestones all day. When I’m helping a new mom navigate her transition, I’m fully present, and that’s a beautiful distraction. It’s the one place where I feel competent and "normal." But there’s a heavy irony there, too. I’m constantly analyzing behaviors and markers, which means my brain is always in "clinical mode." I see a baby with a slight tremor or a mother with a bit of fluid retention, and I have to physically stop myself from projecting my own trauma onto them.

My colleagues are supportive, but they’re also the ones who add this weird layer of pressure. They see me as the "expert," the one who *knows* what’s supposed to happen. They don't see the woman who goes to the bathroom during lunch to check her wedding ring for the tenth time. If I let my guard down and admitted I was scared, I think they’d be shocked. There’s a quiet expectation that because I do this for a living, I should have it all "handled" or "figured out." It’s a very lonely kind of performance, knowing that if I faltered, it would disrupt the image they have of me. I’m just trying to keep the "expert" mask on until the shift ends.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

Every single day. I feel like I’m living two parallel lives that refuse to intersect. In one life, I’m Harriet the expert—the one who guides, observes, and provides calm, grounded advice. I am the lighthouse for other mothers. My identity there is built on competence, knowledge, and stability. 

But then there’s the pregnant me, and that version of myself feels like an imposter. She’s fragile, terrified, and fundamentally broken by a history that my professional peers don't see. When I’m at work, I look at the families I serve and feel a deep, stinging envy. They get to be "just" parents. They get to enjoy the innocence of the process because they haven't had their world tilt on its axis. I feel like I’ve lost that innocence, and in doing so, I’ve lost a piece of the woman I was before the eclampsia.

There’s this constant, gnawing tension between being the one who holds space for everyone else and being the one who desperately needs a space held for her. It’s an exhausting, lonely way to exist. I find myself wondering if I’m losing my identity to the trauma, or if this is just a permanent transformation—that I’ll always be a woman who is looking for the storm, even when the sky is blue.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

I remember one appointment specifically—the one where we hit the halfway point. It’s supposed to be a milestone, a moment of celebration, but all I felt was the clinical coldness of the exam room. The nurse was there, and Mark was sitting on that little plastic stool in the corner, holding his breath as usual. 

The doctor was focused entirely on the screen, moving the ultrasound wand with this practiced, rhythmic efficiency. She was reciting the measurements—"BPD is here, femur length is there"—and it was all so precise, so objective. I felt like a vessel being inventoried. I wanted to scream, *“Does the heart look like it’s struggling? Is there any sign of the stress I feel?”* but I kept my mouth shut. 

Having Mark there made me feel the need to be a "good, calm patient" even more. I knew he was watching the doctor’s face, looking for even a micro-expression of worry. If I had spoken up and said, "I'm terrified that this is all going to disappear," the air would have left the room. So, I lay there, feeling completely exposed and utterly invisible, letting them measure the baby while I carefully tucked my own fear away. The room felt crowded with people, but I have never felt more alone in my life. Everything was "normal," but my body felt like a ticking clock, and no one seemed to hear the beat.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

Honestly, I’ve had to be my own navigator, which is the most frustrating part of all of this. I think people assume that because I work in health-related fields, I have some kind of internal map for the hospital system, but when it comes to my own care, the map is blank.

I handle all the logistics myself—the appointments, the insurance calls, the research into protocols. I don’t trust anyone else to be as thorough as I am. My husband, Mark, is wonderful, but he’s so traumatized by the last time that he’s essentially paralyzed when it comes to decision-making. He defers to me because he’s terrified of making a "wrong" move, which just doubles the pressure on my shoulders.

The system itself makes it harder, though. It’s so fragmented. I feel like I’m constantly translating between being a "professional" and a "patient." The clinic staff treats me like a coworker when I use the right jargon, but they don't provide the guidance a vulnerable pregnant woman actually needs. There isn't a single person—not a midwife, not an OB, not a patient advocate—who has sat me down to say, "Here is the plan to keep you safe given your history." I’m building that plan in the margins of my notebooks, hoping I haven't missed anything vital. It’s lonely, and it’s exhausting.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When we first started talking about this, my mind immediately went back to a midwife I saw years ago—long before the eclampsia. She wasn't just checking boxes; she listened to the rhythm of the conversation. I trusted her because she wasn't in a hurry. She would lean back, ask me how I was *actually* doing, and if I said I was fine, she’d wait until I felt comfortable enough to admit I wasn't. It felt like a partnership.

But that kind of trust feels impossible to replicate now. Because of what happened, I feel like I’ve lost my ability to lean on anyone. I’m constantly evaluating every provider through a lens of "Are you going to notice if I'm failing?" 

I think I’m searching for someone who can hold the weight of my medical history without letting it define the whole experience. I need someone who understands that my "low-risk" status is a clinical fact, not an emotional one. I haven't found that person yet this time around. Everyone is so focused on the *current* metrics—the blood pressure, the urine protein—that they don't see the woman behind the stats. It’s like they want to start my story from the first positive test, but my story started way back in that hospital room, and I’m terrified that if they don't acknowledge the whole history, they’ll miss the signs again.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

That is a difficult question. Looking back, there was definitely a subtle shift after the eclampsia. It’s almost as if my body was reclassified as "defective" or "high-stakes," even when I was perfectly stable between pregnancies. Whenever I’d talk about planning for this one, the conversations felt less like a collaboration and more like a checklist of warnings.

There was one visit with my primary care physician where I brought up the idea of another child. The concern in her eyes wasn't malice, but it felt like a door being slammed shut. She didn’t ask about my desires or how I felt about the risks; she immediately launched into a litany of "what-ifs." It was as if my agency had been replaced by a liability waiver. I felt like a statistic she was trying to manage rather than a person making a life choice.

I think that’s why I’m so guarded now. When you feel like someone is deciding your path for you—or worse, trying to talk you out of your path—you learn to hold your cards close to your chest. I didn't want to be "managed." I wanted to be heard. I wanted someone to sit with the complexity of it all, not just treat my fertility like a ticking time bomb. I’ve realized that the more they try to "steer" me, the more I withdraw.
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

It was just me and my primary care physician. No one else was in the room, but the weight of it felt like there were a dozen people standing there, all nodding in disapproval. She was a doctor I’d seen for years, someone I’d always felt comfortable with, but that day, the air in the room just shifted.

She had my chart open on her screen, and she kept scrolling, her finger stopping on the page where my eclampsia history was noted. She didn't look at me—not really. She was looking at the risk profile. She started using all this clinical language—*maternal morbidity*, *recurrence risk*, *complications*—and it was like she was speaking in a foreign tongue. I felt myself shrinking into the exam chair, turning into a set of numbers she had to "de-risk." 

I remember the silence after she finished her spiel. It wasn't the thoughtful, expectant silence of a partner waiting for me to respond; it was the heavy, final silence of someone who had already reached a conclusion. I felt patronized, honestly. I’m thirty-one, I’m educated, and I know the risks better than anyone because I *lived* them. But she didn't want to hear about my desire to grow our family. She just wanted to categorize me. I left that office feeling like my body wasn't my own anymore—like it was just a piece of equipment she was evaluating for a catastrophic failure. It was the moment I realized that in this system, my "history" can sometimes be a wall that people build between themselves and my humanity.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

Before this pregnancy, my body felt like a place I was just… negotiating a truce with. After the eclampsia, I couldn’t look at it the same way. It felt like a traitor. I spent months checking my hands, my face, my feet, feeling betrayed by the one thing that was supposed to be my home. I wasn’t "in tune" with my body; I was constantly surveying it for signs of revolt. 

I didn't talk about it openly with anyone—not really. How do you explain to a friend, or even your spouse, that you’re frightened of your own skin? If I told them, it would make it real, and I think I spent a lot of time hoping that if I just ignored the fear, it would evaporate. I wanted to believe that I was back to being a "normal" woman, not a "patient-in-waiting."

I felt a lot of pressure to project strength. Everyone around me—my colleagues, my family—sees me as the one who has it all together, the one who helps other mothers navigate their own postpartum journeys. To admit that I felt like my body was a ticking bomb would have felt like a failure of that persona. So, I kept it all in. I exercised, I ate well, I kept my routine perfect, all as a way of trying to bribe my body into behaving this time. It’s a very lonely performance.
Were there parts of your relationship with your body that felt complicated or uncertain as you thought about becoming pregnant?

It wasn't just complicated—it felt like a battle between my heart and my biology. I wanted this baby so badly, but I was terrified that my body would fail me again. I felt like I was holding a volatile chemistry set; I was constantly monitoring my blood pressure, my hydration, my swelling, always waiting for that first sign of "revolt."

My body had become a place of surveillance rather than a place of comfort. I’d look at my reflection and see someone who had been through a medical war, and I’d wonder if I was being selfish or brave for choosing to go back into the fray. I felt a deep, gnawing uncertainty about whether I could trust my own physical signs anymore. Was that headache just dehydration, or was it the start of something dangerous? Was that puffiness in my ankles normal pregnancy weight, or was it the precursor to the seizures?

I didn’t talk about this, because how do you tell your husband, "I want another child, but I’m scared my body will try to kill me"? I felt like I was being disloyal to my own health to want this. It was this constant, quiet tension, and I carried it around like a secret stone in my shoe—always there, always pinching, and always reminding me of the last time things fell apart.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

It’s a bit of a minefield, isn’t it? I have this professional background, so I have a reflex to look for peer-reviewed studies—the clinical, hard data. But when I’m in that headspace of fear, that data starts to feel cold and detached. It tells me the *probability* of eclampsia recurring, but it doesn't tell me how to live through the uncertainty of the daily countdown.

I found myself oscillating between sources. I’d read the ACOG guidelines or articles on Mayo Clinic, thinking, *“Okay, this is factual, this is safe.”* But then, those sources are so sterile. They don’t hold space for the fact that I’m vibrating with anxiety while I read them. 

The "mommy blogs" or forums? They’re the opposite. They have the warmth, the community, but they’re also where I find the horror stories that keep me up at night. I learned quickly to steer clear of the "everything is magical" advice columns—they felt patronizing, like they were ignoring the reality of maternal risk.

Honestly, I didn’t trust any of it completely. I found myself acting like a detective, cross-referencing everything. I’d read a clinical study, then search for a personal account that matched those symptoms, trying to triangulate where my own experience might land. It’s exhausting, and it’s a lonely way to prepare for a journey that’s supposed to be about joy.
Did you ever come across advice or information that felt hard to access — perhaps because of cost, language, or just not knowing where to start?

It wasn't a matter of not being able to find the information—I know exactly which databases to pull up, which journals to search. It was about accessing the *human* side of it. There is no manual for "How to trust your body after it’s betrayed you." 

That kind of help is completely inaccessible. If I search for "post-traumatic stress in pregnancy," I get a million clinical links about medication protocols or screening tools. I don't get a human being who can say, "I know you're terrified. Here is how you can advocate for yourself in the delivery room so you don't feel like you’re losing control again." 

The system treats information like data points—a spreadsheet of risks and mitigation strategies. It doesn't treat it like a conversation that needs to be held with empathy. When you're in the thick of it, the most "accessible" information is often the most useless because it lacks context. I have the medical jargon, sure, but I don't have access to a safe, non-judgmental space where I can put all that jargon aside and just be a scared woman who needs someone to hold her hand. That’s the real gap, isn't it? The information is everywhere, but the *support*—the kind that actually makes you feel safe—that feels like a luxury I can't find.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

Looking back, the "problem" was always my history. It’s like a tattoo on my file that no one can seem to look past. Before I got pregnant this time, I felt like I was constantly auditioning to be a "good enough" candidate for pregnancy. I’d go to appointments for routine checkups, and the moment they saw "Eclampsia" on my chart, the atmosphere would shift. 

It felt like they were waiting for me to be a problem. They’d look at my blood pressure and act like they were expecting it to spike, as if they were disappointed when it stayed normal. It made me feel like my own biology was under constant suspicion. I felt like I was being watched, not cared for. 

I’m thirty-one, which in some circles makes me "advanced" or "high risk," and that just added another layer to the scrutiny. It’s a very specific kind of alienation to feel like your body is a liability in a system designed to look after it. I’d walk out of those offices feeling like I had to prove my own health, as if being a woman who had a complication once meant I’d permanently lost the right to a "normal" pregnancy experience. It makes you hyper-vigilant. You start to see yourself through their lens, and suddenly, you’re not a person anymore—you’re a complication in progress.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

It happened more times than I’d like to admit. There’s a specific kind of dismissive tone some of the specialists use—it’s not unkind, exactly, but it’s completely clinical. They look at the chart, look at my vitals, and their eyes just skate right past me. It’s like I’m a collection of symptoms they’ve seen a thousand times before. 

One day, I tried to mention how anxious I felt about the upcoming third trimester—the time when things went wrong last time—and the response was just a casual, “Well, we’ll monitor you closely, Harriet, but the stats are in your favor.” They meant it to be reassuring, but it felt like they were telling me my feelings were statistically irrelevant. Like my trauma was just a nuisance or a variable to be smoothed out in the data.

I’m standing there, feeling like my life—this baby’s life—is hanging by a thread, and they’re talking about "stats." It’s so invalidating. I want someone to look at me, acknowledge that my fear is the only rational reaction to what I lived through, and then walk me through how we’re going to handle *that* part of the journey. When they reduce me to a risk profile, it makes me feel like I’m already failing, even if the monitor says everything is "normal." It’s the worst kind of loneliness—being in a room full of experts who have no idea how scared you actually are.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

Mark was there for every conversation, but he was always the quiet, anxious shadow in the corner. Before we even started trying, we had long, difficult talks late at night in this very kitchen. He was terrified. He’s seen me go through the physical and emotional wringer, and his biggest fear has always been losing me. 

Because of that, he was involved, but it felt... heavy. Every time we brought up trying again with a doctor, I felt like I was explaining ourselves to a jury. He would hold my hand so tightly his knuckles turned white, and I could tell he was scanning the doctor’s face for any sign of "you're crazy for wanting to do this." 

It shaped everything. I felt like I had to be the "strong one" for both of us, even though I was just as scared as he was. I had to convince the doctor that we were *capable* of handling the risk, which meant I had to downplay my own fears to show that I was "ready." It meant we couldn't just talk about the joy of starting a family. We were stuck talking about complications and mitigation plans, and I think that stole some of the magic from those early days. I wanted him to be my partner in excitement, but we were forced into the roles of patient and cautious protector. It really colored the whole experience of planning—it felt more like preparing for a battlefield than for a new life.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

The moments where my voice mattered felt like rare, brief glimmers of light. I remember one specific conversation with a midwife before I was pregnant; she asked, "What do you need from us to feel safe this time?" It sounds so simple, but it caught me off guard. She wasn't asking for my blood pressure or my medical history—she was asking about my *needs*. In that moment, I felt like a human being again, not just a chart. I told her I needed someone to acknowledge that my fear was valid, not just a "clinical issue." She just nodded, leaned in, and really listened. That felt like true partnership.

But then, there were the times my voice felt completely erased. It usually happened when I’d try to explain how that traumatic birth had permanently changed my relationship with my own body. I’d be talking, pouring out this raw, jagged fear, and I could see their eyes glaze over, checking their watch or turning to the computer screen. They were waiting for me to get to the "relevant" part—the medical stats. 

It was like they were filtering out the "noise" of my emotions so they could get to the "signal" of my diagnosis. It made me feel like I was talking to a wall. You learn pretty quickly that if your voice doesn't align with their protocol, it’s not really a voice at all; it’s just background noise. That’s a crushing realization when you’re literally handing them your life.
When your voice didn't feel heard, what do you think was behind that — was it about who you are, how the system works, or something else?

I think it’s a mix of both, but mostly it’s how the system is built. It’s a conveyor belt, isn’t it? They have these narrow windows of time—ten, maybe fifteen minutes—to assess, document, and move on. There is absolutely no room in that schedule for the messy, nonlinear reality of trauma. When I’m sitting there, trying to tell them that my eclampsia isn't just a bullet point on a past chart but a constant, living shadow, they see it as "noise." They’ve been trained to filter out anything that doesn't fit into the standard clinical boxes. 

It’s also who I am, in a way. Because I’m articulate and work in childcare, they treat me like a peer. They expect me to be objective. They see my background and think, "Oh, she’s one of us, she understands the protocols, she’s fine." They interpret my calm, professional demeanor as a sign that I don't *need* the extra care. They take my competence as permission to ignore my fear. 

It makes me feel invisible. They’re so busy looking for the *data* that they’ve completely stopped looking for the *person*. The system is so rigid that it can’t handle a patient who is both knowledgeable about the medicine and terrified of the experience. It’s like they want me to choose: either be the professional who understands the stats, or be the "hysterical" patient they have to talk down. I’m stuck in this gap where nobody actually hears the woman behind the chart.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

At the start, I think I was just desperate to be "compliant." I thought if I followed every rule, took every supplement, and hit every milestone without a fuss, the system would "reward" me with a safe pregnancy. I was essentially trying to outrun the trauma by being the perfect patient. 

But as the weeks dragged on and I spent more time feeling like a spectator in my own care, that changed. I started to realize that "compliant" just meant "invisible." I’ve begun to push back—not by being aggressive, but by being persistent. When the doctor says, "Everything is fine," I’ve started asking, "How are you measuring that, and what are we looking for in the next few weeks to *keep* it that way?" 

It’s been a shift from seeking their permission to demanding a partnership. I’m starting to understand that I’m the only one who truly carries the weight of this history, so I have to be the one to hold the map. I’ve stopped apologizing for my anxiety. If I need a blood pressure check or an extra minute to discuss a symptom, I ask for it now, regardless of how rushed the room feels. I’m no longer waiting for them to invite me into the conversation about my own body. I’m just taking a seat at the table. It’s terrifying, but it’s the only way I can feel like I have any control left.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

It’s been a bit of a tug-of-war. I remember one appointment where I finally stopped trying to be the "professional colleague" and just told my doctor, "I am not asking for a clinical opinion right now. I am asking for someone to acknowledge that I am terrified." I remember the look on her face—she paused, truly paused, and set her tablet down on the counter. She didn't offer a stat or a reassurance. She just looked at me and said, "I hear you, Harriet. That’s a heavy weight to carry, and I’m sorry you’re having to carry it alone." 

In that moment, I felt like a human being, not a risk assessment tool. She respected that I was the expert on my own fear. It didn't change the medical reality, but it changed how I felt sitting in that chair.

But then, the flip side is brutal. There are other times when I try to voice a concern—like a specific type of headache—and I’m immediately met with a lecture on hydration or stress management, as if I haven't been meticulously tracking every drop of water I drink. They treat my intuition like a nuisance. It’s like they’re waiting for me to hit a "real" medical threshold before they take my words seriously. It makes me feel like I have to wait for an actual emergency to be treated with respect, and that’s a terrifying place to be.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

Mark is physically present for every appointment, standing there like a sentinel, but our dynamic is still so strained by the last time. He’s like a deer in the headlights—he wants to be involved, he *needs* to be involved because he feels as responsible for my safety as I do, but his anxiety is contagious. If he asks a question about the birth plan, I can hear the tremble in his voice. It makes me feel like I’m responsible for holding *his* panic at bay while I’m trying to advocate for myself.

I’ve found myself becoming very guarded about the birth plan. I want him involved, I really do, but I don't want his fear to become a policy. I want a plan that is about empowerment—about keeping my eyes open, about having a voice if I feel that "grey" sensation again—but Mark just wants a plan that ensures I walk out of that hospital alive. Those are two different things, and it creates this quiet friction.

Sometimes I wish I could just have a moment alone with my providers to map things out without worrying about his reaction. It’s hard to have a truly honest conversation about my own boundaries when I’m constantly looking at him, wondering if I’m causing him another night of nightmares. I love that he’s there, I do, but his presence makes the space for "me" feel much smaller.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

Every single time he’s in the room, the dynamic shifts. It’s subtle, but it’s there. When I’m alone with my doctor, I’m sharper—I ask the hard questions about induction timelines or blood pressure thresholds. I’m the woman who wants to hold the map. But when Mark is sitting next to me, his presence acts like a mute button on my anxiety. I find myself sugar-coating my concerns, saying "I’m feeling okay, just a little tired," because if I voice the fear, I can see him physically tense up, gripping his knees. I don't want to be the reason he has a panic attack in the exam room.

And the doctors? They treat us differently, too. They see a united front, a husband-and-wife team, and they fall into this "protect the little family" script. They look at Mark with sympathetic eyes, giving him the "everything is going to be great" reassurance that I know is meant to soothe him, not necessarily address my medical reality. 

It’s like we’re playing a role. We’re the "brave couple expecting again," and by keeping up that facade, we’re actually silencing the most important parts of the conversation. I lose my agency, and he loses his chance to actually process his own trauma. We’re both just nodding along to keep the peace. It’s ironic—his presence is meant to be a support, but it often feels like a barrier to the truth.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

I’ve spent these last few months trying to find a rhythm with my own body, but honestly? It feels more like a hostage situation. Before, I was the one in control—my body did what it was told, I kept it healthy, I knew its limits. Now, it feels like I’m living in a stranger’s skin that’s capable of turning on me at any second. I feel this constant, buzzing anxiety that sits in my stomach alongside the baby. 

I’m not connected to it—I’m *monitoring* it. I watch the swelling, I log the headaches, I track the movements. It’s clinical. I want to feel that beautiful, glowing connection everyone talks about, but I’m too busy playing defense. 

The hardest part is that there’s truly no one I feel safe saying this to. If I tell my friends, they’ll think I’m ungrateful or depressed. If I tell my husband, I’m just pouring gasoline on his own fears. And the doctors? They’d just look at my blood pressure and say, "You're fine, Harriet, everything looks great," which feels like a total dismissal of the internal earthquake I’m living through. I’m utterly alone in this struggle. I keep my "professional" mask polished and shiny for the world, but inside, I’m just holding my breath, waiting for the other shoe to drop.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

It’s the small, casual things that hit the hardest, the ones that are meant to be lighthearted but feel like a punch to the gut. I remember a midwife, early on, who looked at me during a routine check and said, "Oh, look at you—you’re barely showing! You’ve got such a perfect, neat little bump. You’re doing great." 

On the surface, it’s a compliment. Any other woman might have beamed. But for me? It felt like she was grading my pregnancy performance. If I’m "neat" and "little," does that mean I’m winning? Does that mean my blood pressure is being "good" because my body isn't swelling? I felt this immediate, sickening relief that I was "measuring up," followed quickly by a wave of shame because I realized I was using her words to judge my own safety. 

My husband, too, does this thing where he’ll touch my belly and say, "Everything is perfect today, isn't it?" He’s trying to manifest safety, trying to convince himself as much as me. But every time he says it, I feel a desperate need to hide any ache or discomfort I’m feeling. If I tell him I’m exhausted or my head is pounding, I feel like I’m breaking the spell he’s trying to weave. I’m being reduced to a "perfect" vessel, and the pressure to *stay* that way is suffocating. I just want to be allowed to have a body that struggles sometimes without it being a sign of failure.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the thing that hurts the most, and that I wish people understood, is just how much I miss the *wonder* of it. Everyone talks about the miracle of life, the glow of pregnancy, the excitement of the nursery. And for me, that has all been replaced by this relentless, cold calculation. I don’t get to just be a mother-to-be; I’m a manager of risks, a monitor of symptoms, and a silent actress in a play where the stakes are life and death.

I wish the medical system could find a way to treat the *person* alongside the *patient*. I don’t want to be "low risk" or "high risk"—I want to be seen as a woman who has a story, who has trauma, and who needs a hand to hold that isn't just checking a pulse. We focus so much on the physical outcomes—the healthy baby, the surviving mother—that we completely forget about the emotional fallout.

I’m going to go back to my job tomorrow, and I’m going to help other parents navigate their early days with their newborns. I’ll be the expert, the one who tells them, "It’s okay to feel overwhelmed." I just wish I could take my own advice. I wish someone would tell *me* that. I’m just trying to make it to the end without the world going dark again. Thank you for listening to the parts I usually have to keep locked away. It matters.
